title: Peptides & Proteins Quiz
description: Test your understanding of amino acids, peptide bonds, and protein structure

questions.question: How many standard amino acids are encoded by DNA?
questions.explanation: There are 20 standard amino acids encoded by DNA. Despite this small number, they combine to create over 100,000 different proteins in the human body.
questions.hint: Think about how this compares to letters in the alphabet.

questions.question: What type of reaction forms a peptide bond?
questions.explanation: Peptide bonds form through dehydration synthesis (also called condensation). The carboxyl group (-COOH) of one amino acid reacts with the amino group (-NH₂) of another, releasing one water molecule (H₂O).
questions.hint: The reaction releases a small molecule — what comes out when two amino acids join?

questions.question: What makes each amino acid unique?
questions.explanation: The R group (side chain) is what makes each amino acid unique. All amino acids share the same backbone (amino group, alpha carbon, carboxyl group), but each has a different R group that determines its properties.
questions.hint: Three of these are the same in all amino acids — which one varies?

questions.question: Which amino acid type tends to cluster in the interior of proteins, away from water?
questions.explanation: Nonpolar (hydrophobic) amino acids tend to cluster in the protein interior, away from the aqueous environment. This hydrophobic effect is one of the main driving forces of protein folding.
questions.hint: Think about how oil behaves in water.

questions.question: What stabilizes secondary structures like alpha helices and beta sheets?

options.text: Ionic bonds between charged side chains
Hydrophobic interactions between nonpolar side chains

questions.explanation: Secondary structures (α-helices and β-sheets) are stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms — specifically between the C=O of one peptide bond and the N-H of another. Side chain interactions contribute to tertiary structure instead.
questions.hint: Secondary structure is about the backbone, not the side chains.

questions.question: What does primary structure refer to?

options.text: The 3D shape of a folded protein
The linear sequence of amino acids

questions.explanation: Primary structure is the linear sequence of amino acids, from N-terminus to C-terminus. It's determined directly by the gene sequence and is the foundation for all higher levels of structure.
questions.hint: Think of it like reading a sentence — what's the most basic level?

questions.question: Hemoglobin has quaternary structure because it:

options.text: Contains both alpha helices and beta sheets
Has multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) working together
Has a long amino acid sequence

questions.explanation: Quaternary structure refers to the assembly of multiple polypeptide chains (subunits) into a functional complex. Hemoglobin has 4 subunits (2α + 2β chains), each carrying one heme group for oxygen binding.
questions.hint: Not all proteins have quaternary structure — only those with multiple chains.

questions.question: If a protein has 100 amino acids, how many water molecules were released during its synthesis?
questions.explanation: Each peptide bond releases one water molecule. With 100 amino acids, there are 99 peptide bonds (connecting amino acid 1 to 2, 2 to 3, etc.), so 99 water molecules are released.
questions.hint: How many connections are there between 100 beads on a string?
